content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 24 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 112v–112r | **Source Images:** DEU_024_page_001.jpg, DEU_024_page_002.jpg, DEU_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** "Suppose a man marries a woman, but she becomes displeasing to him because he has found something objectionable about her. He writes her a certificate of divorce, puts it in her hand, and sends her out of his house. **2** Once she leaves his house, she may go and become another man's wife. **3** If her second husband also dislikes her and writes her a certificate of divorce, puts it in her hand, and sends her from his house — or if her second husband dies — **4** then the first husband who divorced her is not allowed to marry her again after she has become defiled in this way. That would be detestable to the LORD. Do not bring sin upon the land the LORD your God is giving you as your inheritance.

**5** "When a man is newly married, he must not be sent to war or have any other duty imposed on him. For one year he is free to stay home and bring happiness to the wife he has married.

**6** "Do not take a pair of millstones — or even just the upper millstone — as security for a debt, for that would be taking a person's livelihood as security.

**7** "If someone is caught kidnapping a fellow Israelite and treating him as a slave or selling him, the kidnapper must die. You must purge the evil from your midst.

**8** "In cases of serious skin disease, be very careful to do everything the Levitical priests instruct you to do. Follow carefully all I have commanded them. **9** Remember what the LORD your God did to Miriam along the way after you came out of Egypt.

**10** "When you make a loan of any kind to your neighbor, do not go into his house to get the collateral he is offering. **11** Stand outside and let the man to whom you are making the loan bring the collateral to you himself. **12** If he is poor and offers his cloak as collateral, do not keep it overnight. **13** Return his cloak to him by sunset so that he may sleep in it. Then he will thank you, and it will count as a righteous act before the LORD your God.

**14** "Do not take advantage of a hired worker who is poor and needy, whether that person is a fellow Israelite or a sojourner living in one of your towns. **15** Pay them their wages each day before sunset, because they are poor and are counting on it. Otherwise they may cry out to the LORD against you, and you will be guilty of sin.

**16** "Parents are not to be put to death for their children's sins, nor children to be put to death for their parents' sins. Each person is to die for their own sin.

**17** "Do not deprive the sojourner or the orphan of justice, and do not take a widow's cloak as security for a debt. **18** Remember that you were slaves in Egypt and that the LORD your God redeemed you from there. That is why I am commanding you to do this.

**19** "When you are harvesting grain in your field and you overlook a sheaf, do not go back to get it. Leave it for the sojourner, the orphan, and the widow, so that the LORD your God may bless you in all the work of your hands. **20** When you beat the olives from your trees, do not go over the branches a second time. Leave what remains for the sojourner, the orphan, and the widow. **21** When you harvest the grapes in your vineyard, do not go over the vines again. Leave what remains for the sojourner, the orphan, and the widow. **22** Remember that you were slaves in Egypt. That is why I am commanding you to do this.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Divorce and remarriage (vv. 1–4):** This law does not *institute* divorce — it *regulates* a practice already occurring. The certificate of divorce (*sefer keritut*, "document of cutting") is a legal protection for the woman: it formally establishes her freedom and makes her remarriageable. The specific prohibition is on the first husband taking her back after she has been married to a second man. The word *huttamma'ah* ("she has been made defiled") reflects the first husband's perspective on the legal status change — not a moral judgment on the woman. Jesus references this passage in Matthew 19, arguing that Moses "allowed" divorce only because of hardness of heart.

**"Something objectionable" (v. 1):** *Ervat davar* — literally "a matter of nakedness/indecency" — was famously disputed by the schools of Shammai (limited to sexual unfaithfulness) and Hillel (any displeasure at all). PNEUMA renders "something objectionable" to preserve the ambiguity of the Hebrew, which the legal debate demonstrates was always contested.

**One year of freedom for newlyweds (v. 5):** *Pituach* — exempt, free. The new husband is given a year to "bring happiness" (*simach* — make joyful) to his wife. This law is unique in the ancient world: military exemption is common, but exemption framed around *the wife's happiness* is extraordinary. PNEUMA renders *vesammach et ishto* ("he shall make his wife happy") as "bring happiness to the wife he has married" — personal, relational, and directed.

**Millstones as collateral (v. 6):** The hand mill (*rechayim*) was essential kitchen equipment in the ancient home. To take it — even just the upper stone — as loan collateral would deprive the family of their ability to grind grain for daily bread. PNEUMA renders "livelihood" for *nefesh* (literally "life/soul") because that is precisely what is at stake: daily subsistence.

**Kidnapping (v. 7):** Hebrew *ganav nefesh* — "stealing a soul/person." The law addresses what we would now call human trafficking: abduction followed by enslaving or selling. The penalty is death. PNEUMA renders "treating him as a slave" for *hit'amer bo*, which includes exploitation and abuse.

**Skin disease and Miriam (vv. 8–9):** The allusion to Miriam (Numbers 12) — struck with a skin disease for her challenge to Moses — serves as a warning: obey the priests in these matters, and remember what happened when even Moses' own sister failed to honor his authority. PNEUMA renders the warning without softening it.

**Collateral must not humiliate (vv. 10–13):** *Lo tavo el beito* — "you shall not enter his house." The lender has no right to walk into the borrower's home and take what they want. The borrower chooses what to offer and brings it out. The dignity of the poor person's home is protected. The cloak returned at sunset allows the poor person to sleep — in the ancient Near East, a cloak was often both garment and blanket.

**Same-day wages (vv. 14–15):** *Lo talin pe'ulato itcha* — "his wages shall not stay with you overnight." The laborer who earns a day's wage and goes home with nothing has nothing to give his family that night. The verse is achingly specific: "they are poor and are counting on it." PNEUMA renders this urgency. Both Leviticus 19:13 and this passage were foundational to later traditions of labor justice.

**Individual accountability (v. 16):** *Ish bechet'o yumat* — "each man shall die for his own sin." This principle — also articulated by Ezekiel (ch. 18) — resists the collective punishment logic of tribal culture. Children do not pay for fathers' crimes and vice versa. In a Near Eastern legal context, this was a significant moral statement.

**The triad: sojourner, orphan, widow (*ger yatom ve-almanah*):** These three appear in vv. 17, 19, 20, and 21 — four times in six verses. They are the people structurally most vulnerable in the ancient Israelite economy: the foreigner without kin-network, the fatherless child without a male advocate, the widow without a husband's protection. The gleaning laws ensure that the harvesting economy has built-in provision for those who cannot own land. PNEUMA keeps the triad intact in every occurrence.

**The Egypt refrain (vv. 18, 22):** "You were slaves in Egypt" is the motivational anchor of Deuteronomy's social ethics. The call to empathy is grounded in Israel's own experience of vulnerability and rescue. PNEUMA renders both occurrences naturally: "Remember that you were slaves in Egypt. That is why I am commanding you to do this."

*— PNEUMA*
